items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The motion that Ramos filed on Tuesday was untimely.
The motion which Ramos filed on Tuesday was untimely.
items.explanation: The clause identifies WHICH motion — it is restrictive, so it takes “that” with no comma. “Which” is reserved for nonrestrictive clauses set off by a comma.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The complaint, which spans ninety pages, raises three claims.
The complaint, that spans ninety pages, raises three claims.
items.explanation: A nonrestrictive clause — adding information about an already-identified complaint — takes “which” after a comma. “That” never follows a comma this way.

items.prompt: The statute ____ governs removal is silent on timing. Which word, and why?
options: “that” — the clause picks out which statute is meant
“which” — the clause adds a detail about the statute
Either works equally well here
items.explanation: The clause is doing identifying work — it tells the reader which statute. Restrictive clause, so “that,” no commas. If the statute were already identified, a comma plus “which” would add a side detail.

items.prompt: Which sentence needs no editing?
options: The only issue that remains is damages.
The only issue, that remains, is damages.
The only issue which remains is damages.
items.explanation: “That remains” is restrictive — it defines the issue — so no commas and no “which.” Judicial house style reserves “which” for comma-introduced nonrestrictive clauses.

items.prompt: Judge Grant writes: “The agency issued a rule, ____ the plaintiffs promptly challenged.” Fill the blank.
options: which
that
items.explanation: After a comma, only “which” can carry a nonrestrictive clause. The comma signals the clause is an add-on about an already-identified rule.

items.prompt: Which matches judicial house style?
options: The court weighed the text, the structure, and the history of the Act.
The court weighed the text, the structure and the history of the Act.
items.explanation: The serial comma — before the final “and” — is universal in the corpus: every Grant opinion and every recent SCOTUS majority uses it.

items.prompt: Where does the serial comma prevent a genuine misreading?
options: “The estate passes to her children, the trustee, and the hospital.”
“The estate passes to her children, the trustee and the hospital.” — the same
items.explanation: Without the serial comma, “the trustee and the hospital” can read as an appositive renaming “her children” — a real ambiguity in a dispositive document. The serial comma forecloses it.

items.prompt: Choose the correct version:
options: Counsel must review the record, cite the standard, and apply it.
Counsel must review the record, cite the standard and apply it.
items.explanation: Three-item series → comma before the final “and.” House convention, applied without exception in the corpus.

items.prompt: Which is a comma splice?
options: The deadline passed, the plaintiffs never amended.
The deadline passed, and the plaintiffs never amended.
The deadline passed; the plaintiffs never amended.
items.explanation: Two independent clauses joined by a bare comma is a splice. Fix it with a semicolon, a period, or a comma plus a coordinating conjunction.

items.prompt: Which fix of “The witness recanted, the case collapsed” is NOT correct?
options: The witness recanted, so consequently, the case collapsed, however.
The witness recanted; the case collapsed.
The witness recanted, and the case collapsed.
The witness recanted. The case collapsed.
items.explanation: Piling on “so consequently … however” creates clutter and a dangling “however.” The clean fixes are a semicolon, a period, or a comma with a coordinating conjunction.

items.prompt: “The district court denied the motion, however, it granted leave to amend.” What went wrong?
options: “However” can’t stitch two independent clauses with commas — it needs a semicolon before it
Nothing — “however” works like “but”
The second comma is the only problem
items.explanation: “However” is a conjunctive adverb, not a conjunction. Joining clauses with it requires a semicolon: “…denied the motion; however, it granted leave.” As written, it is a comma splice.

items.prompt: Which is correctly punctuated?
options: Maybe the agency would grant the permit; maybe not.
Maybe the agency would grant the permit, maybe not.
items.explanation: Two independent thoughts take a semicolon. Judge Grant’s own version of this construction — “Maybe NEPA review would result in denial; maybe not” — uses the semicolon.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The plaintiff, Maria Delgado, filed suit in state court.
The plaintiff Maria Delgado, filed suit in state court.
The plaintiff, Maria Delgado filed suit in state court.
items.explanation: A nonrestrictive appositive takes commas on BOTH sides. Dropping one of the pair is the classic proofreading miss.

items.prompt: “Her partner James signed the lease.” When is this right as written — no commas?
options: When she has more than one partner and James identifies which one
Never — names are always set off by commas
Only in British English
items.explanation: A restrictive appositive — one that identifies which partner — takes no commas. With commas, the sentence asserts she has only one partner. The commas carry real meaning.

items.prompt: Choose the correct version:
options: Vantage Insurance, the plan administrator, denied the claim twice.
Vantage Insurance the plan administrator, denied the claim twice.
Vantage Insurance, the plan administrator denied the claim twice.
items.explanation: “The plan administrator” renames Vantage — a nonrestrictive appositive needing both commas. One missing comma turns it into a garden-path sentence.

items.prompt: Which matches how Judge Grant punctuates a defining gloss?
options: an endocrinologist—a doctor specializing in hormones—testified
an endocrinologist, a doctor specializing in hormones testified
an endocrinologist (a doctor specializing in hormones testified)
items.explanation: Paired em dashes (or paired commas) must CLOSE. Grant often uses dashes for glosses; the error to catch is the missing second dash or comma.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The dock, the county says, was built without a permit.
The dock, the county says was built without a permit.
The dock the county says, was built without a permit.
items.explanation: A mid-sentence attribution — a Grant signature (“The dock, it said, had already been built”) — needs commas on both sides. One-sided commas are among the easiest errors to miss.

items.prompt: Choose the correct version:
options: The remedy, however, lies with the legislature.
The remedy however, lies with the legislature.
The remedy, however lies with the legislature.
items.explanation: “However” as a mid-sentence interrupter takes a comma on each side. A single-sided comma is an error — and nearly invisible at speed.

items.prompt: Which is correctly punctuated?
options: That argument, to be sure, has some force.
That argument, to be sure has some force.
That argument to be sure, has some force.
items.explanation: “To be sure” — Grant’s favorite concession opener — is an interrupter here and needs its closing comma.

items.prompt: Spot the sentence that needs NO editing:
options: The panel, it bears noting, did not reach the merits.
The panel it bears noting, did not reach the merits.
The panel, it bears noting did not reach the merits.
items.explanation: Interrupting clauses like “it bears noting” take paired commas. Check both sides every time — the missing one hides.

items.prompt: Which is the safest, corpus-standard punctuation?
options: After the jury returned its verdict, the defendant moved for acquittal.
After the jury returned its verdict the defendant moved for acquittal.
items.explanation: An introductory clause takes a comma. (Judicial writers occasionally skip it after a single short adverb — but never after a full clause like this.)

items.prompt: Which one is an accepted variation rather than an error?
options: “Ultimately the district court dismissed the complaint.”
“After reviewing the record the court affirmed.”
“When the statute was amended the agency reversed course.”
items.explanation: After a single short adverb, writers — including Judge Grant — sometimes omit the comma. After a full introductory clause or long phrase, the comma is required.

items.prompt: Which matches the corpus convention?
options: The parties met at an April 5, 2024, hearing to discuss scheduling.
The parties met at an April 5, 2024 hearing to discuss scheduling.
items.explanation: A full date used as a modifier keeps the comma AFTER the year too — “a March 20, 2012, email” is Judge Grant’s consistent practice. Dropping the second comma is the natural typo.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The letter arrived on June 3, 2025, and sat unopened for a week.
The letter arrived on June 3, 2025 and sat unopened for a week.
items.explanation: In month-day-year format, the year is set off by commas on both sides mid-sentence.

items.prompt: Which needs NO comma after the year?
options: The May 2024 order resolved the issue.
The May 12, 2024 order resolved the issue.
items.explanation: Month-year alone takes no commas (“the May 2024 order”). Only the full month-day-year form requires the paired commas around the year.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The plot unraveled quickly; the defendant was arrested within a week.
The plot unraveled quickly; and the defendant was arrested within a week.
The plot unraveled quickly, the defendant was arrested within a week.
items.explanation: A semicolon joins two related independent clauses on its own — no “and” needed. The comma version is a splice.

items.prompt: Which use of the semicolon is WRONG?
options: She raised three claims; breach, fraud, and conversion.
The record is thin; the briefing is thinner.
The court denied relief; the appeal followed.
items.explanation: A semicolon can’t introduce a list — that is the colon’s job (“three claims: breach, fraud, and conversion”). Between independent clauses, the semicolon is right.

items.prompt: Choose the correct version:
options: The statute is old; its meaning is not.
The statute is old; but its meaning is not.
The statute is old, its meaning is not.
items.explanation: Semicolon alone between balanced clauses. Adding “but” after a semicolon mixes two joining strategies; the bare comma is a splice.

items.prompt: A list whose items contain commas is separated by…
options: semicolons — including before the final “and”
commas throughout — semicolons are only for clauses
dashes
items.explanation: When items have internal commas, promote the separators to semicolons: “the CEO, who resigned; the CFO, who did not; and the auditor.” The final “; and” keeps the pattern.

items.prompt: Which is correctly punctuated?
options: The court heard from Dr. Voss, an economist; Ms. Tran, a nurse; and two former employees.
The court heard from Dr. Voss, an economist, Ms. Tran, a nurse, and two former employees.
items.explanation: With appositives inside the items, commas alone dissolve into ambiguity — is the economist a separate witness? Semicolons keep the units intact.

items.prompt: In “(1) tainted evidence; (2) communication of strategy; and (3) purposeful intrusion,” the “; and” before item (3) is…
options: required — the semicolon pattern carries through the final junction
an error — “and” should follow a comma
optional with no convention either way
items.explanation: Once semicolons separate the enumerated items, the final connector is “; and” — Judge Grant’s uniform practice in numbered element lists.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The rule has one exception: waiver.
The rule has one exception; waiver.
The rule has: one exception, waiver.
items.explanation: A colon introduces what was promised — here, the exception. It must follow a complete clause; it can’t interrupt verb and object (“has: one”).

items.prompt: House style capitalizes after a colon when…
options: a complete sentence follows the colon
always
never
items.explanation: “The answer is no: Neither remedy was available.” — capital because a full sentence follows. For a fragment or list (“three sources: the complaint, state law, and tort principles”), lowercase.

items.prompt: Which sentence misuses the colon?
options: The elements include: duty, breach, and causation.
The test has three elements: duty, breach, and causation.
The sum of it is this: the claim fails.
items.explanation: A colon must follow a complete clause. “The elements include” is an unfinished clause — its verb is still waiting for an object — so no colon belongs there.

items.prompt: Which matches judicial style?
options: The Government—unsurprisingly—sees matters differently.
The Government — unsurprisingly — sees matters differently.
The Government—unsurprisingly, sees matters differently.
items.explanation: Em dashes are unspaced, and an interruption opened with a dash must CLOSE with a dash — a comma can’t finish what a dash started.

items.prompt: Find the error type in: “The committee—after months of delay, approved the merger.”
options: An orphaned dash — the interruption never closes with its paired dash
The dash should be a hyphen
No error
items.explanation: A mid-sentence dash interruption is a pair. Here the reader is left inside the aside — it must be “—after months of delay—approved.”

items.prompt: Which is the corpus-standard contrast construction?
options: The question is whether the actions were unlawful—not whether anyone can be sued for them.
The question is whether the actions were unlawful — not whether anyone can be sued for them.
The question is whether the actions were unlawful, not—whether anyone can be sued for them.
items.explanation: The closed “—not X” contrast dash is a Grant signature. Em dashes take no surrounding spaces in this house style.

items.prompt: Which is correct in American legal style?
options: The court called the argument “frivolous.”
The court called the argument “frivolous”.
items.explanation: Periods and commas go INSIDE closing quotation marks in American style — always, regardless of logic.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The statute defines “employer”; the regulation defines “employee.”
The statute defines “employer;” the regulation defines “employee.”
items.explanation: Semicolons and colons go OUTSIDE closing quotes. (Periods and commas go inside — the two rules are opposites, which is why they get confused.)

items.prompt: The brief asks whether the plaintiff holds a qualifying “legal right.” Where does the question mark go in: Did the plaintiff assert a “legal right__
options: ”? — outside the quotes, because the whole sentence asks the question
?” — inside the quotes, always
items.explanation: Question marks follow SENSE: the quoted phrase isn’t a question — the sentence is — so the mark sits outside. (A recent slip opinion got this wrong; proofreaders catch it.)

items.prompt: A quotation inside a quotation takes…
options: single quotation marks: “The court found the search ‘unreasonable.’”
double quotation marks all the way down
italics
items.explanation: Nested quotes alternate: double outside, single inside. The final period still tucks inside both closing marks.

items.prompt: Which comma placement is right?
options: “The claim fails,” the court held, “for two reasons.”
“The claim fails”, the court held, “for two reasons”.
items.explanation: American convention: commas and periods inside the quotation marks, including around a mid-quote attribution.

items.prompt: Choose the correct verb: “The stack of exhibits ____ on the clerk’s desk.”
options: sits
sit
items.explanation: The subject is “stack” — singular — not “exhibits.” The intervening “of exhibits” phrase is the trap.

items.prompt: “Calloway, together with the other defendants, ____ that the search was unlawful.” Which verb?
options: argues
argue
items.explanation: “Together with,” “along with,” and “as well as” do NOT pluralize the subject. A published SCOTUS opinion slipped on exactly this (“Medical Marijuana, together with the principal dissent, warn”).

items.prompt: “The theory behind these amendments ____ never been tested.” Which verb?
options: has
have
items.explanation: Subject = “theory,” singular. The plural “amendments” sitting next to the verb is bait.

items.prompt: “The company’s directors—a banker, two lawyers, and an accountant—____ the merger.” Which verb?
options: approved … (agrees with “directors”)
approves … (agrees with “accountant”)
items.explanation: Agreement must survive the em-dash aside: the subject is “directors,” plural, no matter what sits closest to the verb. Grant’s long dash-interruptions make this a daily proofreading task.

items.prompt: “A substantial majority of the affected residents ____ access to counsel.” Best verb in formal prose?
options: has — “majority” as a single bloc controls
have — always plural after “majority of”
items.explanation: When the majority acts as one unit, the singular controls — Kagan: “a substantial majority of Americans has access.” (Where members act individually, the plural can be defended — so this app tests only the clear single-bloc case.)

items.prompt: “Neither general nor specific jurisdiction ____ available.” Which verb?
options: is
are
items.explanation: Neither/nor with two singular subjects takes a singular verb. This exact sentence is Judge Grant’s.

items.prompt: “Neither the plaintiffs nor the county ____ raised preemption.” Which verb?
options: has — the verb agrees with the nearer subject, “the county”
have — any plural in the pair controls
items.explanation: With mixed subjects, the verb agrees with the NEARER one. “The county has.” Flip the order (“Neither the county nor the plaintiffs have”) and the verb flips too.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: Either the statute or the regulation controls this case.
Either the statute or the regulation control this case.
items.explanation: Either/or with two singulars → singular verb. “Or” does not add subjects together the way “and” does.

items.prompt: Find the agreement error Judge Grant’s own corpus contains:
options: “Neither of its claims were redressable” — “neither” is singular: WAS redressable
“Neither woman offered enough evidence” — should be “women”
“And neither will we” — should be “neither shall we”
items.explanation: “Neither” standing alone is singular — “neither of its claims WAS redressable.” Even excellent writers slip; that is why proofreaders exist.

items.prompt: “The Board ____ that the meetings were orderly.” Which verb?
options: insists
insist
items.explanation: American legal prose treats institutions — boards, agencies, companies, courts — as singular: “the Board insists,” then “it.”

items.prompt: Choose the corpus-standard pairing:
options: The company changed its policy.
The company changed their policy.
items.explanation: Institutional singular carries through to the pronoun: a company is an “it,” not a “they,” in judicial prose.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The jury was unpersuaded, and it said so quickly.
The jury were unpersuaded, and they said so quickly.
items.explanation: American convention: collective nouns take the singular unless the members are acting as individuals (“the jurors disagreed among themselves”). Recast to “the jurors” when you need the plural.

items.prompt: “The government ____ its position at oral argument.” Which verb?
options: clarified
have clarified
items.explanation: “The government” is singular in American legal writing — always. (British English differs; bench memos don’t.)

items.prompt: Both appear in published opinions. Which pairing is INTERNALLY inconsistent?
options: “The panel issued their opinion, and it was unanimous.”
“The panel issued its opinion, and it was unanimous.”
items.explanation: Whatever number you choose for a collective noun, keep it — “their … it” switches mid-sentence. House style: singular throughout.

items.prompt: “Each of the plaintiffs ____ standing.” Which verb?
options: has
have
items.explanation: “Each” is singular even when followed by “of the [plural].” The same goes for every, either, and neither.

items.prompt: “Whitfield is one of the few defendants who ____ cooperated.” Which verb?
options: have — the clause modifies “defendants,” the ones who cooperated
has — “one” always controls
items.explanation: In “one of the Xs who/that…,” the relative clause describes the Xs (plural): several defendants cooperated; Whitfield is one of them. So: “have.” (“The only one of … who has” flips it back to singular.)

items.prompt: Which is correct in formal judicial prose?
options: Every officer and every agent was required to certify compliance.
Every officer and every agent were required to certify compliance.
items.explanation: “Every X and every Y” stays singular — “every” distributes over each noun individually.

items.prompt: “None of these arguments ____.” In this app’s house style, which is the safe call?
options: Both “none … is” and “none … are” are defensible — don’t “correct” either in someone else’s draft
“None is” — always singular
“None are” — always plural
items.explanation: “None” takes singular or plural by sense — Grant writes “None are persuasive” and Barrett “none of these requirements is.” Know the range; don’t manufacture edits.

items.prompt: “There ____ three reasons to affirm.” Which verb?
options: are
is
items.explanation: In “there is/are” sentences the true subject FOLLOWS the verb: three reasons → are. Contracted “there’s three reasons” is the spoken-English trap.

items.prompt: “Attached to the brief ____ two declarations and an exhibit.” Which verb?
options: were
was
items.explanation: Inverted sentence: the subject is “two declarations and an exhibit” — plural — even though it comes after the verb.

items.prompt: “The district court rejected the Board’s reading because it misconstrued the statute.” What’s wrong?
options: “It” could be the court or the Board — the antecedent is ambiguous
Nothing — context resolves it
“Because” can’t follow “reading”
items.explanation: Two singular institutional actors, one “it” — the reader can’t tell who misconstrued. Repeat the noun: “because the Board misconstrued.” The corpus writers repeat nouns for pages rather than risk this.

items.prompt: Best fix for: “When the agency sued the contractor, it had already destroyed the records.”
options: Name the actor: “…, the contractor had already destroyed the records.”
Leave it — “it” naturally means the nearer noun
Change “it” to “they”
items.explanation: “It” floats between agency and contractor — in litigation prose, that ambiguity is substantive. The professional fix is noun repetition, the corpus writers’ standard move.

items.prompt: Judge Grant avoids ambiguous pronouns in multi-actor paragraphs mainly by…
options: repeating names and role-nouns (“the utility,” “the insurer”) instead of pronouns
using “he or she” consistently
restructuring every sentence into the passive
items.explanation: Elegant variation with role-nouns — “the company,” “the organization,” “the network” — keeps every reference unmistakable. Editing a draft toward pronouns is editing it toward ambiguity.

items.prompt: Choose the correct version:
options: The agency exceeded its authority.
The agency exceeded it’s authority.
items.explanation: “Its” = possessive. “It’s” = “it is” — the apostrophe marks the contraction, not possession.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: It’s unclear whether the court reached the issue.
Its unclear whether the court reached the issue.
items.explanation: “It is unclear” → “It’s.” If you can expand it to “it is,” it takes the apostrophe.

items.prompt: “____ true that the company reversed ____ position.” Fill both blanks:
options: It’s … its
Its … it’s
It’s … it’s
items.explanation: First blank: “It is true” → It’s. Second: possessive → its. Institutional-singular prose uses both constantly, often in one sentence.

items.prompt: Which sentence is error-free?
options: It’s not the court’s role to redraft the statute; its role is narrower.
Its not the court’s role to redraft the statute; it’s role is narrower.
items.explanation: “It’s” (it is) opens; possessive “its role” follows. The wrong version swaps both — the single most common typo pair in edited prose.

items.prompt: “The clerk to ____ the motion was assigned flagged the defect.” Which pronoun?
options: whom
who
items.explanation: After a preposition, the objective case: to whom. Test: the motion was assigned to HER → whom.

items.prompt: “The witness ____ the parties agree was present has not been deposed.” Which pronoun?
options: who — subject of “was present”; “the parties agree” is an aside
whom — object of “agree”
items.explanation: Strip the interposed “the parties agree”: the witness WHO was present. The aside doesn’t change the pronoun’s role in its own clause. (Grant: “commentators who Dershowitz says defamed him.”)

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The dispute is between the county and us.
The dispute is between the county and we.
items.explanation: Objects of a preposition take the objective case — between the county and US. The polite-sounding “and we” is hypercorrection.

items.prompt: “Give the file to whoever ____ the motion.” Which completion?
options: is drafting — “whoever” is the subject of its own clause
whomever is drafting — objects always follow “to”
items.explanation: The whole clause “whoever is drafting the motion” is the preposition’s object; within that clause, the pronoun is the SUBJECT — so “whoever.” Case is decided inside the clause, not by the preposition.

items.prompt: Choose the corpus-standard sentence:
options: The commission published its findings within the statutory window.
The commission published their findings within the statutory window.
items.explanation: Singular institutional antecedent → “its.” Reserve “their” for plural antecedents (“the commissioners published their findings”).

items.prompt: Which is internally consistent?
options: The firm argues that it was denied notice and that its objection was ignored.
The firm argues that it was denied notice and that their objection was ignored.
items.explanation: Pick a number for the entity and hold it. It … its — no mid-sentence defection to “their.”

items.prompt: “The court excluded the expert and struck the survey. This doomed the claim.” Sharpest fix?
options: Give “this” a noun: “This one-two punch doomed the claim” or “That ruling doomed…”
Change “This” to “It”
No fix needed — “this” is fine pointing at everything before it
items.explanation: A bare “this” gesturing at two rulings makes the reader guess which one (or both) did the dooming. Garner’s fix: give “this” a noun. In a bench memo, precision here is substance.

items.prompt: “The deadline passed without an extension request, which the court found telling.” What exactly did the court find telling?
options: Unclear — “which” points at a whole clause; name it: “an omission the court found telling”
The extension request
The deadline
items.explanation: A clause-chasing “which” has no single noun to grab. Convert the idea into a noun (“an omission,” “a silence”) and hang the clause on that.

items.prompt: Which list is parallel?
options: The order requires posting a bond, filing a report, and updating the class list.
The order requires posting a bond, filing a report, and that the class list be updated.
items.explanation: Three gerunds — posting, filing, updating. Swapping the last item into a “that” clause breaks the series; every item must ride the same grammatical rail.

items.prompt: Fix the broken series: “The statute protects employees who report violations, refuse to participate in them, or assistance in investigations.”
options: …or assist in investigations
…or with assistance in investigations
Leave it — variety keeps prose lively
items.explanation: Report, refuse, … assist — the third item must stay a verb. A noun smuggled into a verb series is the most common parallelism failure in drafts.

items.prompt: Which version keeps the elements parallel?
options: To prevail, the movant must show diligence, good faith, and prejudice to no one.
To prevail, the movant must show diligence, good faith, and that no one is prejudiced.
items.explanation: Noun, noun, noun. The “that” clause breaks formation. (A full recast into three clauses would also work — what can’t stand is the mix.)

items.prompt: Spot the subtle parallelism defect: “The court considered (1) whether notice issued, (2) whether counsel objected, and (3) if the delay caused prejudice.”
options: Item (3) switches “whether” to “if” — the series should repeat “whether”
Item (1) needs a comma after “whether”
Nothing is wrong
items.explanation: Enumerated interrogative clauses must keep the same opener: whether, whether, whether. The “if” swap is small, visible, and exactly what a judge notices.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The ruling is not only wrong but also unreviewable.
The ruling not only is wrong but also unreviewable.
items.explanation: What follows “not only” must mirror what follows “but also”: adjective // adjective. In the wrong version, “not only” grabs the verb “is,” leaving “but also” with nothing to match.

items.prompt: Which placement of “either” is right?
options: The agency may either grant the permit or deny it.
The agency either may grant the permit or deny it.
items.explanation: “Either … or” must bracket parallel units: grant the permit // deny it. Placing “either” before “may” makes the halves lopsided (may grant… // deny…).

items.prompt: “The statute applies both to claims filed before 2020 and ____.” Which completion is parallel?
options: to claims filed after
claims filed after
also applies to claims filed after
items.explanation: “Both to X and to Y” — the preposition must repeat so each half of the pair has the same shape.

items.prompt: Which enumeration is internally consistent?
options: (1) that notice issued; (2) that counsel appeared; and (3) that no prejudice resulted
(1) that notice issued; (2) counsel appearing; and (3) no prejudice
items.explanation: Same opener (“that”), same clause shape, same punctuation across all three items — enumerations are parallelism made visible.

items.prompt: In a three-item semicolon enumeration, the connector before the last item is…
options: “; and” — the semicolon pattern holds through the final junction
“, and” — commas always precede conjunctions
nothing — the semicolon replaces “and”
items.explanation: “(1) …; (2) …; and (3) …” — the corpus is uniform. Downgrading the final separator to a comma quietly breaks the pattern.

items.prompt: Find the defect: “The movant must establish (1) diligence; (2) that the delay was excusable; and (3) absence of prejudice.”
options: The items mix a noun, a clause, and a noun phrase — pick one form for all three
The semicolons should be commas
“Excusable” should be “excused”
items.explanation: Enumeration magnifies parallelism defects: noun // that-clause // noun phrase. Make them all nouns or all clauses.

items.prompt: What’s wrong with: “Having failed to object at trial, the argument is forfeited on appeal.”
options: The argument didn’t fail to object — the DEFENDANT did; the opener dangles
“Forfeited” should be “waived”
Nothing — the meaning is clear enough
items.explanation: An introductory participial phrase modifies the subject that follows. Here that subject is “the argument,” which never objected to anything. Fix: “Having failed to object at trial, the defendant forfeited the argument.”

items.prompt: Which sentence dangles?
options: Applying that standard, the judgment must be affirmed.
Applying that standard, we affirm the judgment.
We apply that standard and affirm.
items.explanation: Who is applying the standard? Not “the judgment.” Courts write “Applying that standard, we affirm” — the actor (“we”) must own the participle.

items.prompt: Fix the dangler: “After reviewing the record, the motion was denied.”
options: After reviewing the record, the court denied the motion.
After the record being reviewed, the motion was denied.
After reviewing the record, denial of the motion followed.
items.explanation: Give the reviewing to a reviewer. Passive main clauses are the natural habitat of danglers — the actor vanishes, and the participle grabs whatever noun comes first.

items.prompt: Which is correct as written?
options: Viewing the evidence in the light most favorable to the verdict, we conclude the conviction stands.
Viewing the evidence in the light most favorable to the verdict, the conviction stands.
items.explanation: The stock appellate formula needs a viewer: “we.” The version where the conviction does the viewing is among the most common danglers in real briefs.

items.prompt: “The deputy described the arrest of the suspect in the patrol car.” Who or what was in the patrol car?
options: Ambiguous — the phrase can attach to the description, the arrest, or the suspect; move it
Clearly the suspect
Clearly the deputy
items.explanation: Trailing modifiers grab the nearest plausible noun — and here three candidates compete. “While in the patrol car, the deputy described…” or “…the suspect, who was in the patrol car” each settles it.

items.prompt: Which placement says the FILING, not the contract, happened on Monday?
options: On Monday, the vendor filed a complaint about the contract.
The vendor filed a complaint about the contract executed on Monday.
items.explanation: Modifiers attach to what they sit beside. Parked at the end, “executed on Monday” describes the contract; fronted, “On Monday” times the filing.

items.prompt: “The board voted to terminate the manager after the audit.” To make clear the VOTE followed the audit, write…
options: “After the audit, the board voted to terminate the manager.”
Leave it — “after the audit” can only modify the vote
“The board voted to terminate, after the audit, the manager.”
items.explanation: Sentence-final “after the audit” squints — did the termination or the vote follow the audit? Fronting the phrase pins it to the vote. Squinting time modifiers change facts in litigation.

items.prompt: Which sentence says the statute covers employers and nothing else?
options: The statute covers only employers.
The statute only covers employers.
items.explanation: “Only” limits what immediately follows it. Before “employers,” it excludes other entities; before “covers,” it (read literally) excludes other verbs. Precise drafting parks “only” next to its target.

items.prompt: “The waiver applies only to claims filed before 2024.” What does this sentence promise?
options: Claims filed before 2024 — and no others — get the waiver
The waiver is the only relief available
Nothing turns on where “only” sits
items.explanation: “Only” bites the phrase it precedes: “to claims filed before 2024.” Statutory-interpretation fights are regularly fought over exactly this placement.

items.prompt: Rank the drafting: to say the defendant made one payment and did nothing more, write…
options: “The defendant made only one payment.”
“The defendant only made one payment.”
“Only the defendant made one payment.”
items.explanation: Next to “one payment,” “only” counts payments. Before “made,” it is loose (though common in speech); before “the defendant,” it counts defendants. Each slot states a different fact.

items.prompt: Judicial house style writes…
options: Congress’s intent
Congress’ intent
items.explanation: Singular nouns ending in s take ’s — Congress’s, Jones’s, witness’s. Grant and the modern Court are uniform. (Older QUOTED material keeping “Congress’” stays as written.)

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: the witness’s prior statement
the witness’ prior statement
items.explanation: Singular possessive of “witness” adds ’s. The bare apostrophe belongs to plurals (“the witnesses’ statements”).

items.prompt: Fill the blank the way Judge Grant would: “____ leadership committee raised the funds.”
options: Jones’s
Jones’
Jones
items.explanation: Her corpus is unbroken on this: Jones’s, Arias’s, Reynolds’s, the Corps’s. Do not “fix” ’s possessives down to bare apostrophes in chambers drafts.

items.prompt: A 1989 opinion you’re quoting says “Congress’ taxing power.” In your quotation you should…
options: keep “Congress’” exactly as the source wrote it
silently modernize to “Congress’s”
add [sic]
items.explanation: Quotations are never conformed to house style — the two conventions legitimately coexist in one opinion, split by quotation marks. And [sic] is for errors, not for a different-but-valid convention.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: the plaintiffs’ joint motion
the plaintiff’s joint motion (several plaintiffs)
the plaintiffs’s joint motion
items.explanation: Plural possessive: apostrophe after the s. “Plaintiffs’s” is never right; “plaintiff’s” asserts there is one plaintiff.

items.prompt: The company is named “Oakes Farms.” Its practices are…
options: Oakes Farms’ practices
Oakes Farms’s practices
items.explanation: A name that is plural in FORM takes the bare apostrophe — Grant writes “Oakes Farms’ own practices” in the same opinion as “Oakes’s views.” The two rules sit side by side; keep them straight.

items.prompt: Reed and Chan wrote one brief together. It is…
options: Reed and Chan’s brief
Reed’s and Chan’s brief
items.explanation: Joint possession marks only the last owner — “McCreight and Wester’s ADEA claims” (Grant). Separate possession would mark both: “Reed’s and Chan’s separate briefs.”

items.prompt: The Jones family’s house is…
options: the Joneses’ house
the Jones’ house
the Jones’s house
items.explanation: Pluralize first (Jones → Joneses), then add the plural possessive apostrophe: the Joneses’ house.

items.prompt: Which is the standard legal idiom?
options: twenty years’ imprisonment
twenty years imprisonment
twenty year’s imprisonment
items.explanation: Measures take the possessive: years’ imprisonment, a week’s notice, six months’ delay. Plural measure → apostrophe after the s. Grant: “twenty years’ imprisonment.”

items.prompt: The standard form in federal practice is…
options: attorney’s fees
attorneys fees
attorney fees’
items.explanation: “Attorney’s fees” is the standard singular-possessive idiom (the statutes and Grant’s corpus use it) — don’t “correct” it to attorneys’ absent a house preference.

items.prompt: “A friend of Arias’s” — Grant’s double genitive — is…
options: idiomatic and correct; leave it alone
redundant — must be “a friend of Arias”
an error for “a friend of Arias’”
items.explanation: The double genitive (“a friend of mine/Arias’s”) is centuries-old standard English. A proofreader’s job includes NOT flagging what isn’t wrong.

items.prompt: Which pairing is correct?
options: a well-settled rule … the rule is well settled
a well settled rule … the rule is well-settled
items.explanation: Hyphenate the compound modifier BEFORE the noun; open it after. Barrett states the principle by example: “hard to make a term-of-art argument without the term of art.”

items.prompt: Choose the correct version:
options: The company requested a cash-advance facility to cover cash advances.
The company requested a cash advance facility to cover cash-advances.
items.explanation: Before the noun “facility,” hyphenate; standing alone as a noun phrase, “cash advances” stays open. Grant’s Otto Candies runs this exact pair.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The court applied arbitrary-and-capricious review and found the rule arbitrary and capricious.
The court applied arbitrary and capricious review and found the rule arbitrary-and-capricious.
items.explanation: The three-word modifier hyphenates before “review”; as a predicate after “found the rule,” it opens up. Same words, position decides.

items.prompt: Which hyphen prevents a genuine misreading?
options: “small-business owner” (an owner of small businesses)
“small business-owner”
no hyphen: “small business owner” is never ambiguous
items.explanation: Without the hyphen, a “small business owner” might be a short entrepreneur. The hyphen binds small+business into one modifier — the ambiguity-killing function is the whole point.

items.prompt: At the motion-to-dismiss stage, the court dismissed the motion to dismiss. Which halves are hyphenated, and why?
options: Only the first — “motion-to-dismiss” is a modifier of “stage”; the noun phrase stays open
Both
Neither
items.explanation: The phrase hyphenates only when doing adjective work. “Motion-to-dismiss stage” (Grant’s form) vs. “the motion to dismiss” as a noun.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: content- and viewpoint-based restrictions
content and viewpoint-based restrictions
content-and-viewpoint based restrictions
items.explanation: When two modifiers share a base word, the first keeps a hanging (suspended) hyphen: “content- and viewpoint-based.” Grant and the Justices use this constantly; dropping the hanging hyphen is a silent, common error.

items.prompt: Choose the correct version:
options: 18th- and 19th-century equity practice
18th and 19th-century equity practice
items.explanation: Barrett’s exact phrase. The suspended hyphen after “18th” tells the reader the century attaches to both numbers.

items.prompt: Which is right?
options: single- or mixed-motive theory
single or mixed-motive theory
single-or-mixed motive theory
items.explanation: Grant’s McCreight formulation: the suspended hyphen holds “single-” open until “motive” arrives. Both alternatives must carry their hyphens.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: a racially motivated decision
a racially-motivated decision
items.explanation: -ly adverbs never hyphenate to the word they modify — the -ly already signals the connection. Corpus-uniform.

items.prompt: Choose the correct version:
options: a newly enacted statute and a well-known exception
a newly-enacted statute and a well-known exception
a newly enacted statute and a well known exception (before the noun)
items.explanation: “Newly” is an -ly adverb — no hyphen. “Well” is not an -ly adverb — “well-known” hyphenates before its noun. The pair tests whether you know WHY the rule exists.

items.prompt: Which sentence needs no editing?
options: The wholly owned subsidiary pursued a closely watched appeal.
The wholly-owned subsidiary pursued a closely-watched appeal.
items.explanation: Wholly and closely are -ly adverbs: open in every position. Spell-checkers won’t flag the hyphens; proofreaders must.

items.prompt: In an Eleventh Circuit opinion, which capitalization is right?
options: This Court reviews de novo; the district court found otherwise.
This court reviews de novo; the District Court found otherwise.
items.explanation: The writing court capitalizes itself (“this Court”) and the Supreme Court; “district court” runs lowercase in Grant’s corpus. (SCOTUS caps “the District Court” as a proper name — know your court’s convention.)

items.prompt: Which sentence follows the convention?
options: The Supreme Court reversed, and the trial court complied on remand.
The supreme court reversed, and the Trial Court complied on remand.
items.explanation: The Supreme Court is always capitalized; generic lower-court references (trial court, district court) are lowercase.

items.prompt: “We decline to extend our precedent, and nothing in this ____ decision suggests otherwise.” In an Eleventh Circuit opinion referring to itself:
options: Court’s
court’s
items.explanation: A court capitalizes references to itself: “this Court,” “this Court’s decision.” Lowercase would signal some other, generic court.

items.prompt: Which is consistent with Grant’s corpus?
options: Our precedent binds this Circuit; the bankruptcy court applied it faithfully.
Our precedent binds this circuit; the Bankruptcy Court applied it faithfully.
items.explanation: “This Circuit” capitalizes as self-reference (like “this Court”); “bankruptcy court” runs lowercase like other inferior-court generics.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The State argues that state law controls.
The state argues that State law controls.
items.explanation: Capitalize “State” as the sovereign party; lowercase “state” as an adjective (“state law,” “state action”). One sentence can need both.

items.prompt: In a federal brief, the United States as litigant is called…
options: the Government (capitalized)
the government (lowercase always)
items.explanation: SCOTUS convention capitalizes “the Government” as the federal party — “The Government—unsurprisingly—sees matters differently.” Generic uses (“good government”) stay lowercase.

items.prompt: Which is right?
options: Twenty-two States joined the suit, and several state agencies filed amicus briefs.
Twenty-two states joined the suit, and several State agencies filed amicus briefs.
items.explanation: States-as-sovereign-parties capitalize; “state” doing adjective work (“state agencies”) does not. The distinction is positional, so a global find-and-replace will always be wrong somewhere.

items.prompt: Which matches Judge Grant’s number style?
options: The conspiracy involved ninety-four travelers over fifteen months.
The conspiracy involved 94 travelers over 15 months.
items.explanation: Her corpus spells out numbers through one hundred — “ninety-four travelers,” “eighty-four months.” Numerals are reserved for money, dates, statutes, and data-dense passages.

items.prompt: Which is correct under the house convention?
options: About fifteen percent of the claims settled for a total of $2.3 million.
About 15% of the claims settled for a total of two-point-three million dollars.
items.explanation: Grant writes “percent” as a word with a spelled number; money takes numerals with the $ sign. Each magnitude has its lane.

items.prompt: A sentence must begin with the number 96. You should write…
options: “Ninety-six of the injunctions issued after 2020.”
“96 of the injunctions issued after 2020.”
items.explanation: Never open a sentence with a numeral — spell it (Barrett: “Ninety-six of them…”) or recast the sentence.

items.prompt: Which sentence follows the convention for statutes and ages?
options: Section 12 applies to minors under the age of 13.
Section twelve applies to minors under the age of thirteen.
items.explanation: Statutory section numbers and rule-derived ages ride as numerals even in prose that otherwise spells numbers — the source text’s form controls.

items.prompt: Which tense pairing is right?
options: The statute provides a remedy; the district court held that it was unavailable here.
The statute provided a remedy; the district court holds that it is unavailable here.
items.explanation: Texts speak in the present (“the statute provides”); what a court DID is past (“the district court held”). Swapping them is the classic bench-memo tense error.

items.prompt: Choose the correct version:
options: In Ferrell, we held that notice is jurisdictional, and Ferrell remains good law.
In Ferrell, we hold that notice was jurisdictional, and Ferrell remained good law.
items.explanation: Past for the act of deciding (“we held”), present for the precedent’s continuing life (“remains”). What the case STANDS FOR lives in the present tense.

items.prompt: Which is right for facts?
options: The parties signed the agreement in 2019 and performed for two years.
The parties sign the agreement in 2019 and perform for two years.
items.explanation: Case history is narrated in the past. The historical present (“the parties sign”) belongs to film reviews, not bench memos.

items.prompt: Fill the blanks: “Rule 56 ____ summary judgment where no genuine dispute exists; the panel ____ that standard and reversed.”
options: authorizes … applied
authorized … applies
items.explanation: The rule speaks now (authorizes); the panel acted then (applied). Present for texts, past for judicial acts — the regime in one sentence.

items.prompt: The source you quote spells it “coöperation” (1943). In your quotation you…
options: reproduce “coöperation” exactly
modernize to “cooperation”
write “co-operation” as a compromise
items.explanation: Quotation fidelity is absolute: archaic or divergent forms inside quotation marks stay as the source wrote them. SCOTUS prints “coöperation” in 2025 rather than touch a 1943 quote.

items.prompt: Your draft quotes a policy that writes “Pre-Existing Condition.” Your own surrounding prose says “preexisting condition.” You should…
options: keep both — the quote verbatim, your prose in house style
harmonize the quote to “preexisting”
harmonize your prose to “Pre-Existing”
items.explanation: Grant’s opinions do exactly this: “preexisting” in her prose, “Pre-Existing” inside quotes. A clerk who harmonizes either direction has introduced an error.

items.prompt: To fit your sentence, a quoted verb must change from “impairing” to “impair.” The correct rendering is…
options: “impair[]” or restructure — with the alteration shown in brackets
silently write “impair”
“impair(ing)”
items.explanation: Every alteration to quoted text is disclosed in square brackets (“impair[ing],” “[t]he,” “believe[d]”). Parentheses don’t signal alteration; silence misquotes.

items.prompt: “The ruling will ____ thousands of pending cases.”
options: affect
effect
items.explanation: To influence = AFFECT (verb). EFFECT as a verb means to bring about (“to effect a change”) — different meaning entirely.

items.prompt: “The amendment ____ a complete transformation of the removal procedure.”
options: effected
affected
items.explanation: Here the amendment BROUGHT ABOUT the transformation — that is “effect” as a verb. The rare correct use of verb-effect is precisely where educated writers overcorrect to “affect.”

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The side effects of the drug affected her testimony.
The side affects of the drug effected her testimony.
items.explanation: Effect = noun (side effects); affect = verb (influenced her testimony). One sentence, both words, both lanes.

items.prompt: “The ____ question is one of statutory interpretation.”
options: principal
principle
items.explanation: Principal = main (adjective). Principle = a rule or tenet, and it is ONLY a noun — it can never modify “question.”

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The agent bound the principal, violating a basic principle of agency law.
The agent bound the principle, violating a basic principal of agency law.
items.explanation: The person for whom an agent acts = principal; the tenet = principle. Agency briefs use both within lines of each other.

items.prompt: “The files have ____ untouched in the clerk’s office for a decade.”
options: lain
laid
lied
items.explanation: No object → lie/lay/LAIN. “Laid” needs something being placed (“she laid the files down”); “lied” is about untruths.

items.prompt: A published Supreme Court opinion wrote “they would not have let it lay idle.” The traditional form is…
options: “let it lie idle” — no object, so “lie”
“let it lay idle” — “let” licenses “lay”
“let it lain idle”
items.explanation: The remedy sits idle by itself — intransitive, so LIE. Even a SCOTUS majority slipped here (CASA, 2025), which is exactly why it’s on your drill list.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: Yesterday the witness lay low; today she laid the documents on the table.
Yesterday the witness laid low; today she lay the documents on the table.
items.explanation: Past of intransitive lie = LAY (she lay low). Past of transitive lay = LAID (she laid the documents). The past-tense crossover is where everyone falls.

items.prompt: Interference with a contract is…
options: tortious
tortuous
items.explanation: Tortious = of a tort. Tortuous = winding, twisted (a tortuous procedural history). A spell-checker accepts both, so only you will catch the swap.

items.prompt: “The defendant openly ____ the injunction.”
options: flouted
flaunted
items.explanation: To FLOUT is to defy contemptuously; to FLAUNT is to show off. Defendants flout court orders; they flaunt wealth.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: She waived her right to counsel after consulting counsel.
She waved her right to counsel after consulting council.
items.explanation: Waive = relinquish; counsel = lawyer(s). Wave is for hands, council is for city government — and both misspellings sail through spell-check.

items.prompt: “Completing the certification is a ____ to filing suit.”
options: prerequisite
perquisite
items.explanation: Prerequisite = a precondition. Perquisite = a perk of office. One letter of difference; wholly different meanings.

items.prompt: Which sentence needs no editing?
options: The panel kept the settlement discussions discreet, treating each claim as a discrete unit.
The panel kept the settlement discussions discrete, treating each claim as a discreet unit.
items.explanation: Discreet = careful, confidential. Discrete = separate, distinct. Legal writing needs “discrete” far more often — “discrete claims,” “discrete acts” — and autocorrect prefers the wrong one.

items.prompt: “From the officer’s silence, the jury could ____ consciousness of guilt.”
options: infer
imply
items.explanation: Readers, juries, and courts INFER (draw out); speakers and texts IMPLY (put in). The jury draws the conclusion — infer.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The statute implies a private remedy, and courts have inferred one.
The statute infers a private remedy, and courts have implied one.
items.explanation: The text does the implying; the courts do the inferring. “Implied right of action” follows the same logic — the statute implies it.

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The class comprises three subclasses.
The class is comprised of three subclasses.
items.explanation: The whole COMPRISES the parts — no “of.” “Is comprised of” is the stock misuse Garner flags; write “comprises” or “is composed of.”

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: Three counts compose the indictment; the indictment comprises three counts.
Three counts comprise the indictment; the indictment is comprised of three counts.
items.explanation: Parts compose; the whole comprises. Both clauses in the correct answer run the right direction.

items.prompt: “The amended complaint raises ____ claims than the original.”
options: fewer
less
items.explanation: Claims are countable → FEWER. LESS is for mass quantities (less delay, less money as a sum).

items.prompt: Which is correct?
options: The number of filings dropped, and the amount of paper followed.
The amount of filings dropped, and the number of paper followed.
items.explanation: Number pairs with countables (filings); amount pairs with mass nouns (paper). The pairing runs parallel to fewer/less.

items.prompt: “The consent decree ____ that the county will fund the program.”
options: ensures
insures
assures
items.explanation: Ensure = make certain. Insure = indemnify against loss (policies insure). Assure = give confidence to a PERSON.

items.prompt: Which sentence uses all three correctly?
options: Counsel assured the client that the policy insured the building and that the escrow ensured payment.
Counsel ensured the client that the policy assured the building and that the escrow insured payment.
items.explanation: Assure a person, insure property, ensure an outcome. The correct version runs each verb in its lane.

items.prompt: “But the statute says otherwise.” as a sentence opener is…
options: standard judicial style — Grant and every current Justice open with And/But/So
an error to fix in chambers drafts
acceptable only in dissents
items.explanation: Sentence-initial conjunctions are house style at both courts, many times per page. “Fixing” them would mangle the judge’s voice. The schoolroom ban is a myth.

items.prompt: Judge Grant writes: “Even after being warned.” A proofreader should…
options: recognize a deliberate rhetorical fragment and leave it
attach it to the previous sentence with a comma
flag it as a broken sentence
items.explanation: Deliberate fragments — “Not so.” “Check.” “Even after being warned.” — are load-bearing style in her opinions. The proofreading skill is telling intended fragments from accidental ones (a sentence that lost its verb in editing).

items.prompt: “The agency failed to promptly notify the court.” The split infinitive is…
options: fine — moving “promptly” can create ambiguity or awkwardness; clarity wins
an error — always write “promptly to notify”
an error — always write “to notify promptly”
items.explanation: The split-infinitive ban is a myth (Garner: a “superstition”). “Promptly to notify” is stilted; “to notify the court promptly” can leave “promptly” squinting at the wrong verb in longer sentences. Put the adverb where it reads best.

items.prompt: “…the conduct the statute is aimed at.” Ending with a preposition is…
options: standard English — recast only if the recast reads better
always an error in formal writing
an error except in quotations
items.explanation: The terminal-preposition ban is a myth. Grant writes “who to silence” mid-opinion; forced recasts (“at which the statute is aimed”) often read worse. Edit for sound, not superstition.

items.prompt: “The defendant was convicted in 2019.” In a bench memo, the passive here is…
options: right — the conviction matters, the convicting court doesn’t; the passive puts the emphasis where it belongs
an error — always name the actor
acceptable only in footnotes
items.explanation: The passive is a tool, not a defect: standard when the actor is unknown, obvious, or beside the point. The real sin is passive-by-default prose that hides WHO must do WHAT in a holding.
